topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed a monotonous tune, mirroring the rhythm of my anxiety. Sarah, the department head, leaned back in her ergonomic chair, a knowing, almost pitying smile playing on her lips. "I need you to walk Alex through the onboarding process," she stated, her voice smooth as polished glass. My stomach immediately clenched. Walking someone through everything felt like being handed the keys to a machine I hadn't fully mastered, knowing that if I stumbled, the whole system would grind to a halt. I nodded, the movement stiff. I spent the next three days meticulously reviewing every protocol, my fingers tracing the procedural manuals repeatedly. Every question Alex asked—simple queries about filing systems or software navigation—felt like a spotlight illuminating my inadequacy. When Alex finally looked up, a flicker of genuine confusion in his eyes, I felt a sharp, unpleasant tightening in my chest. I just wanted to disappear into the beige carpet.

[story 2]

"Take the reins now, Mark," the manager instructed, tapping a pen against the desk. The air in the conference room suddenly felt thick, pressurized. I watched as Alex took a deep breath, trying to project confidence, but his shoulders were visibly slumped. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to shrink into my seat, to become invisible. Every instruction I offered felt amplified, scrutinized. I kept replaying the training scenarios in my head, trying to construct a perfect narrative for him. When he asked a question that required a nuanced understanding of a long-forgotten client file, my mind went completely blank. A bead of sweat trickled down my temple. I mumbled something vague, feeling the heat rise in my neck. I desperately hoped he wouldn't notice the tremor in my hands as I reached for the correct document.

[story 3]

The sheer weight of expectation settled on my shoulders like a physical cloak. I could feel the gaze of everyone in the room—Sarah, Alex, even the silent cubicle walls—all focused on my performance. "Just show him the ropes," Sarah encouraged, her tone implying this was a simple handover. I started speaking, my voice sounding strangely thin and reedy. I moved through the steps mechanically, reciting procedures rather than explaining concepts. When Alex paused, waiting for clarification on a complex database query, I froze. The silence stretched, agonizingly long. My palms grew slick against the edge of the presentation screen. I could hear my own heartbeat drumming loudly in my ears. I desperately wished the floor would just open up and swallow me whole.
